concepts.name: Glossary
concepts.explanation:  Compiler – a program that translates from a source code (a readable code)  into a machine code (binary code that is understand by a specific architecture  – x86, x64, ARM, etc)  A compiler can be:  Native – the result is a native code application for the specific architecture  Interpreted – the result is a code (usually called byte-code) that requires an  interpreter to be executed. Its portability depends on the portability of its  interpreter

concepts.name:
Executable

Glossary
concepts.explanation:  Linker – a program that merges the object files  obtained from the compiler  phase into a single executable  It also merges various libraries to the executable that is being create.  Libraries can be linked in the following ways:  Dynamically: When application is executed, the operating system links it with the  necessary libraries (if available). If not, an execution error may appear.  Static: The resulted executable code contains the code from the libraries that it  uses as well

code_examples.title: Pointer vs Reference

concepts.name: Pointers and References
concepts.explanation:  The resulted code is identical (both the “Pointer” and “Reference” program  will link into the same assembler code.  However, from the programmer point of view, using a reference fixes some  possible problems (perhaps the most know one is that one does not need to  use the “->” operator – instead the “.” operator can be used). Another  important one is that a check for NULL pointers is no longer required.

concepts.name: Pointers and References
concepts.explanation:  References and pointers are created in the following manner:  The difference is that pointers can remain uninitialized. This forces the programmer to initialize a reference.  It also guarantees that a reference points to a valid memory location.

code_examples.title: Pointer vs Reference Behavior

concepts.name: Pointer Reference
concepts.explanation: uninitialized reference

concepts.name: Pointers and References
concepts.explanation:  A pointer value can be changed (that is a pointer can point to different  memory addresses). A reference can only point to a variable and once it is  initialize the memory address where it points to can not be changed.  A pointer can have the value NULL.

code_examples.title: Pointer Arithmetic

concepts.name: Pointer Reference
concepts.explanation: &refI = j; Compiler error – trying  to change a reference  that was already  initialized.

concepts.name: Pointers and References
concepts.explanation:  Pointers accept certain arithmetic operations (+, - , ++, etc). This is not valid  for references.  In case of pointers, variable “i” and “j” are allocated consecutively on the  stack. The operation “p++” moves the pointer p from the memory address of  the variable “i” to the memory address of the variable “j”. At the end of the  execution “j” will have the value 30.

code_examples.title: Reference Limitations

concepts.name: Pointer Reference

concepts.name: Initialization lists
concepts.explanation:  “{“ and “}” can be used for array initialization as well:  If possible, the compiler tries to deduce the size of the array from the  declaration. If the initialization list is too small, the rest of the array will be  filled with the default value for that type (in case of “int” with value 0 → values that are grayed in the table). void main() { int x[3] = { 1, 2, 3 };

concepts.explanation:  “{“ and “}” can be used to initialize a matrix as well.  However, only the first dimension of the matrix can be left unknown. concepts.name:
Constructors

Constructors
concepts.explanation:  A constructor is a type-less function that is called whenever a class is created.  A class may contain multiple constructors (with different initialization  parameters)  A class does not need to have a constructor. However, if it has at least one,  then it’s initialization should be based on that constructor parameters.  If one class contains several member data that have their own constructors,  those constructors will be called in the same order of their declaration.   A constructor can not be static or constant

concepts.name: Constructors
concepts.explanation:  The constructor is defined as a function with the same name as the class  and no return value  A class : o May have NO constructors o May have only one constructor o May have multiple constructors o May have constructors that are not public   MyClass ();

concepts.name: Type of constructors
concepts.explanation: Constructors:  Default constructor (without any parameters)  Copy constructor  Move constructor A class can have none, one , some or all of these types of constructors.   MyClass ();   MyClass (const MyClass & objToCopyFrom);   MyClass (const MyClass && objToMoveFrom);

concepts.name:
Destructor

Destructor
concepts.explanation:  A destructor function is called whenever we want to free the memory that an  object occupies.  A destructor (if exists) is only one and has no parameters.  A destructor can not be static  A destructor can have different access modifiers (public/private/protected). Date::Date() : x(100) { ... } Date::~Date() { ... } void main()

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Destructor
concepts.explanation:  The most common usage of the destructor is to deallocate the memory that  has been allocated within the constructor or other functions. { char * text; String(const char * s) { text = new char[strlen(s) + 1]; memcpy(text, s, strlen(s) + 1); { delete text; text = nullptr;

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Destructor
concepts.explanation:  This code will not compile. The destructor of class Date is private. Date::Date() : x(100) { ... } Date::~Date() { ... } void main()  error C2248: 'Date::~Date': cannot access private member  declared in class 'Date' note: compiler has generated 'Date::~Date' here note: see declaration of 'Date''

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Destructor
concepts.explanation:  This code will compile – because the destructor will (even if private) is not be  called (the object is created in the heap memory and it is never deallocated). Date::Date() : x(100) { ... } Date::~Date() { ... } void main()   Date *d = new Date();

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Destructor
concepts.explanation:  This code will not compile because “delete d” call will attempt to use a  private destructor. Date::Date() : x(100) { ... } Date::~Date() { ... } void main()   Date *d = new Date();  error C2248: 'Date::~Date': cannot access private member  declared in class 'Date'

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Destructor
concepts.explanation:  This code will compile. The destructor is private , but it can be access by a  method from its class (In this case DestroyData).       static void DestroyData(Date *d); Date::Date() : x(100) { ... } Date::~Date() { ... } void Date::DestroyData(Date *d) void main()   Date *d = new Date();

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Destructor
concepts.explanation: Let's consider the following class: The destructor is called when: A. When program ends, for every global variable B. When a function/method ends for every local variable C. When the execution exists a scope (for variable defined within a specific scope) D. When the delete operator is called over a heap allocated instance     ~Date() { printf("dtor was called \n"); }    for (int tr=0;tr<10;tr++) {

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Destructor
concepts.explanation:
 Objects are destroyed in the reverse order of their creation (similar to the  way a stack works → first created is the last destroyed).     ~Tree() { printf("dtor: Tree
"); }     ~Car() { printf("dtor: Car
"); }     ~Animal() { printf("dtor: Animal
"); }     ~Date() { printf("dtor: Date
"); } void main()  dtor: Animal

concepts.name:
Inheritance

Inheritance
concepts.explanation:  Inheritance is a process that transfer class proprieties (methods and  members) from one class (often called the base class to another that inherits  the base class – called derived class). The derive class may extend the base   Such an example will be the class Automobile, where we can define the  following properties:  Number of doors  Number of wheels  From this class we can derive a particularization of the Automobile class (for

concepts.name: Inheritance
concepts.explanation:  Inheritance in case of C++ classes can be simple or multiple:  Simple Inheritance  Multiple Inheritance  The access modifier is optional and can be one of the following: (public / private or protected).   If it is not specified, the default access modifier is private.

concepts.name:
Casts

Casts
concepts.explanation:  Assuming that a class A is derived from a class B, than it is possible for  an object of type A to be converted to an object of type B.  This is a normal behavior (obviously A contains every member defined   The conversion rules are as follows: ❖ It will always be possible to convert a class to any of the classes that it  ❖ It is not possible to convert from a base class to one of the classes that  inherits it without an explicit cast ❖ If the cast operator is overwritten, none of the above rules apply.

concepts.name: STL (Standard Template Library)
concepts.explanation:  A set of templates that contains:  Containers (class templates that may contain other classes)  Iterators (pointer used for template iteration)  Algorithms (functions that can be called for a container)  Adaptors  Allocators  To use an STL template, we have to include the header(s) where that  template is defined.

code_examples.title: Example 1
code_examples.code:
class templates that may contain other classes)



concepts.name: STL- Containers
concepts.explanation:  Sequence containers  forward_list  Adaptors (stack, queue, priority_queue)  Associative containers  Ordered: set, multiset, map, multimap  Un-ordered: unordered_set, unordered_map, unordered_multiset,  unordered_multimap

code_examples.title: STL Containers Overview

concepts.name:
Sequence 
containers

STL - Vector
concepts.explanation:  Vector = an unidimensional array for objects  Constructors:  Memory allocation / resizing is done dynamically  Every object added in a vector is actually a copy of the original one using namespace std; void main(void)   vector<Type> v;   vector<Type> v(Size);

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: STL - Vector
concepts.explanation:  Insertion in a vector is done using the following commands: push_back, insert  For deletion, we can use: pop_back, erase or clear  For reallocation: the resize and reserve methods  For access to elements: the index operator and the “at” method using namespace std; void main(void)   vector<int> v;   v.push_back(1);v.push_back(2);

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: STL - Vector
concepts.explanation: After execution: i=6,v[0]=5 using namespace std;   Integer() : Value(0) {}   Integer(int v) : Value(v) {}   Integer(const Integer &v) : Value(v.Value) {}   void Set(int v) { Value = v; } void main(void)   vector<Integer> v;

Integer() : Value(0) { printf("[%p] Default ctor
", this); }   Integer(int v) : Value(v) { printf("[%p] Value ctor(%d)
", this,v); }   Integer(const Integer &v) : Value(v.Value) { printf("[%p] Copy ctor from (%p,%d)
",                                                      this, &v, v.Value); }   Integer& operator= (const Integer& i) { Value = i.Value; printf("[%p] op= (%p,%d)
",                                                this, &i, i.Value); return *this; }   void Set(int v) { Value = v; } void main(void)

concepts.name: STL (associative containers - map)
concepts.explanation:  Map is a container that store pairs of the form: key/value; the access to the  value field can be done using the key  For use “#include <map>”  The key field is constant: after a key/value pair is added into a map, the key  cannot be modified - only the value can be modified. Other pairs can be  added and existing pairs can be deleted.  The declaration of map template: template < class Key, class Value, class Compare = less<Key> > class map

concepts.name: Lambda expressions

concepts.name:
Basic Exceptions

Exceptions
concepts.explanation:  Exceptions are a way to identify errors that can occur in a program and react  Where exception-1,2,…3 could be anything: a type, a reference, a pointer catch (<exception-1>) { … } catch (<exception-2>) { … } catch (<exception-3>) { … } catch (…) { /* the rest of the possible exceptions */ }

code_examples.title: Try-Catch Basics

concepts.name: Exceptions
concepts.explanation:  Additionally, the keyword throw can be used to throw an exception that will  further be received by the try … catch block When using this method, the object <obj> will be send to the catch block (as  such the <exception> parameter in the catch block should have the same type or  a type where there is a possible conversion from the thrown type exists.     throw <obj> catch (<exception> /* exception has the same type as obj */ ) { … } catch (…) { /* the rest of the possible exceptions */ }

code_examples.title: Multiple Exception Types

concepts.name: Exceptions
concepts.explanation:  Let’s see an example: for (int tr = 0; tr < 10; tr++) { if (tr == 5) throw "Reached 5"; catch (const char* error) { std::cout << "Error: " << error; Error: Reached 5

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Exceptions
concepts.explanation:  A more complex example: for (int tr = 0; tr < 10; tr++) {             if (tr == 5) throw "Reached 5"; if (tr == 3) throw 3; if (tr == 1) throw true; std::cout << "Number : " << tr << std::endl; catch (const char* e) { std::cout << "String Err: " << e << std::endl; } catch (int e) { std::cout << "Int Err: " << e << std::endl; }

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Exceptions
concepts.explanation:  However, the rules of promotion don’t apply:  The throw instruction sends a char, but the catch requires an int. As such the  exception is not caught and a runtime crash will happen. for (int tr = 0; tr < 10; tr++) {             if (tr == 3) throw 'A'; std::cout << "Number : " << tr << std::endl; catch (int e) { std::cout << "Int Err: " << e << std::endl; }

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Exceptions
concepts.explanation:  However, you can always use catch(…) to capture any kind of exception.  Details related to the exception will not be present. for (int tr = 0; tr < 10; tr++) {             if (tr == 1) throw true; if (tr == 3) throw 'A';  if (tr == 5) throw "some error"; if (tr == 7) throw 10; std::cout << "Number : " << tr << std::endl;

code_examples.title: Example 1

concepts.name: Generic exceptions
concepts.explanation: Int Err: 10

concepts.name: Single Responsibility Principle
concepts.explanation:  A class should have only one reason to change, focusing on a single  responsibility Advantages:  Easier to understand.  Lower coupling between logic and infrastructure.  Improved testability and reusability.
